what_it_does: Acceptance test that verifies decision_view reconstructs a complete, chronologically ordered lineage for a surfaced decision even when the log contains far more than 999 later unrelated events.
observations: Creates a temporary FsStore and discovers nodes from the nodes directory
Surfaces a decision, emits early grow and frame events tagged with the surfaced id, then emits 1200 unrelated noise events
Resolves the surfaced decision with a reason
Reads the full event tail via store.events_since(-1) and checks the log depth exceeds 999
Calls suite.decision_view(sid) and inspects the trajectory kinds, order, surfaced tags, and resolution reason
Uses a module-level PASS counter and a check helper that prints ok for each passing assertion
standouts: Deliberately buries early lineage under 1200 unrelated events to test the old 999-event window behavior
Asserts the earliest proposal event survives at the start of the returned trajectory
Explicitly links decision_view behavior to session_view full-tail reading
conventions: Docstring states the law/invariant being tested before the code
Uses tempfile with a finally cleanup block
Self-contained acceptance test with no external test framework imports
Breaks typical encapsulation by calling suite._emit directly from a test
concerns: Relies on the private suite._emit method, so it is sensitive to internal API changes
Global mutable PASS counter makes parallel execution unsafe
Assumes exact semantics of the surfaced tag and decision_view response shape without defining them
notes: A fail-loud acceptance test that proves decision lineage audits are not silently truncated by a newest-first window.
questions: What do the grow, frame, and noise event kinds mean in the domain model?
How is decision_view implemented, and does it always read the full tail or only when a surfaced tag is present?
Is the 999-event window still used anywhere else in the codebase?
What is the contract of suite.resolve_surfaced and the shape of its emitted resolve event?
purpose_vs_actual: Yes — the stated purpose of preventing silent truncation matches the actual sequence of setup and assertions.
intention_signals: To provide a behavioral proof that decision_view reads the full relevant event history rather than a newest-first window, so early lineage events cannot vanish from an audit.
novelty: It uses a 1200-event burial scenario to empirically verify that a lineage view returns the whole history of a long-lived decision.
contribution: An acceptance test that encodes the invariant that a decision lineage view must return the complete, chronologically ordered, decision-scoped event history including the resolution reason, without silently truncating early events.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance test that surfaces a decision, buries its early events under 1200 unrelated log entries, resolves it, and asserts decision_view returns the full chronological lineage including the original proposal, framing, and resolution reason.
intention_for_embedding: To prove decision_view reads the full event tail like session_view and never silently truncates a long-lived decision's audit lineage.

symbols.does: Assertion helper that increments a global pass counter and prints an ok message.
symbols.description: A lightweight test utility that takes a label and a condition, asserts the condition with a prefixed failure message, increments the module-level PASS counter on success, and prints a confirmation line. It gives the acceptance test a self-reporting check style without relying on an external test framework.
